Course Title: AI in Advertising: Truth, Bias, and Manipulation
Category: Media & Entertainment: Publishing, Film, Advertising & Communications, Gaming
Description: Ethical risks in targeting and messaging.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how advertising works (such as awareness of online ads, social media marketing, or traditional campaigns) is all you need to get started, as no technical or AI background is required.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key ethical risks associated with AI-driven advertising, explain how algorithmic bias can influence targeting and messaging, and recognise the techniques used to manipulate consumer behaviour. You will also apply practical frameworks to evaluate AI-generated advertising content critically, helping you make more informed and responsible decisions within the advertising and communications industry.

lessons.lesson title: AI in Advertising: Truth, Bias, and Manipulation
lessons.Description: Advertising has always relied on persuasion, but artificial intelligence is transforming the scale and sophistication of how audiences are targeted and influenced. This lesson explores the ethical risks that arise when AI is used to craft messages, personalise content, and reach specific groups of people with pinpoint accuracy. You will learn how algorithmic bias can creep into advertising systems, leading to unfair or discriminatory outcomes that may go unnoticed by the brands responsible. We will also examine how AI-powered tools can blur the line between helpful personalisation and manipulative tactics that exploit human psychology. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of what responsible, transparent advertising looks like in an age where AI is doing much of the work behind the scenes.
lessons.content: Ethical risks in targeting and messaging.

slides.subtitle: Exploring ethics, truth, and targeting risks.
slides.slidecontent:
• AI is reshaping how adverts are created, targeted, and personalised at scale
• Real ethical risks exist
• Advertisers can now reach specific audiences with unprecedented precision
• This raises serious questions about truth, fairness, and manipulation
• Beginners welcome, no prior knowledge needed

slides.subtitle: The mechanics behind personalised advertising
slides.slidecontent:
• AI analyses browsing habits, purchase history, and location data
• Algorithms identify likely buyers based on digital footprints
• Precision targeting reduces wasted advertising spend
• Systems match specific demographics with relevant products
• Real-time decisions determine which ads appear when

slides.subtitle: When algorithms exclude and discriminate
slides.slidecontent:
• Training data reflects existing societal inequalities
• Certain groups get excluded from job, housing, or financial ads
• Bias emerges without deliberate programming
• Underrepresented communities may be systematically overlooked
• Discrimination can be invisible to advertisers themselves

slides.subtitle: When algorithms detect and target weakness
slides.slidecontent:
• AI identifies patterns of grief, stress, and emotional distress
• Targeted ads appear during vulnerable moments
• Search behaviour reveals personal struggles
• Precision exploitation scales to millions instantly
• Emotional manipulation masquerades as personalisation

slides.subtitle: Real harm from predatory advertising
slides.slidecontent:
• Gambling ads follow addiction recovery searches
• Debt products pushed to financially struggling families
• Children exposed to inappropriate adult content
• Mental health crises exploited for profit
• Age-inappropriate targeting systems fail safeguards

slides.subtitle: Why permission isn't truly informed
slides.slidecontent:
• Terms and conditions are deliberately complex and lengthy
• Users rarely understand what data they're sharing
• Consent is buried in legal jargon
• Opt-out processes are hidden or difficult
• True informed consent is practically impossible

slides.subtitle: Safeguards, regulations, and consumer power
slides.slidecontent:
• UK Online Safety Act and GDPR limit AI profiling practices
• Right to opt out of personalised advertising exists
• Transparency requirements for AI-generated content
• Independent audits can identify harmful bias
• Clear complaint mechanisms provide redress routes

slides.subtitle: What to remember and do
slides.slidecontent:
• AI targeting is precise and data-driven, never random
• Bias emerges from imperfect training data
• Personalisation involves deliberate choices about what you see
• Always question who benefits from persuasive ads
• Review your platform targeting settings as a practical first step

scripts.script: Welcome, and thank you for joining this course on AI in Advertising. Over the next few lessons, we are going to explore something that is genuinely reshaping the world around us. Artificial intelligence is now deeply embedded in how adverts are created, how they are targeted, and how they are personalised, often at a scale that was simply not possible just a few years ago. And while that brings some remarkable opportunities, it also brings some very real risks. We are talking about questions of truth, fairness, and whether some of these techniques cross a line into manipulation. Now, if those words sound a little serious, do not worry. This course is designed to be accessible to everyone, so no prior knowledge is needed whatsoever. Whether you work in marketing, you are just curious about how this all works, or you want to be a more informed consumer, you are in exactly the right place. So let us get started.

Let's start by understanding how AI targeting actually works behind the scenes. When you see an advert online, it hasn't appeared by accident. AI systems constantly analyse your browsing history, what you've bought before, and even where you are, to figure out who is most likely to be interested in a product. This creates highly specific audience profiles that allow advertisers to reach exactly the right people at exactly the right time. The precision is remarkable – rather than showing an advert to everyone and hoping for the best, these systems can identify likely buyers with extraordinary accuracy. This means advertising budgets go much further because companies aren't wasting money on people who simply aren't interested. It sounds efficient and beneficial, but this foundation of data-driven targeting is where both the opportunities and the ethical challenges begin.

Now here's where things get problematic. Those same targeting systems can quietly exclude certain groups from seeing opportunities, whether that's job adverts, financial products, or housing. And often, nobody has deliberately programmed that exclusion. It creeps in through the data the AI has been trained on – data that reflects existing inequalities in society. If historical hiring patterns show bias against certain communities, the AI will learn and perpetuate those patterns. The system can only work with the data it's given, and if that data doesn't represent everyone equally, then certain groups will be systematically overlooked. This creates a troubling cycle where AI doesn't just reflect existing discrimination – it amplifies and automates it, often without the advertisers even realising it's happening.

This is where things get genuinely uncomfortable. Modern advertising algorithms are extraordinarily good at detecting when you are emotionally vulnerable. If you have been searching for information about bereavement, illness, or financial stress, the system notices. And rather than leaving you alone during difficult times, these patterns become targeting opportunities. The AI can identify emotional states from search behaviour, social media activity, and browsing patterns, then serve highly specific ads at precisely the moment you are least equipped to think critically. What makes this especially troubling is that emotional manipulation can now happen at massive scale – what might have been a limited problem in the past can now reach millions of people almost instantly, with each person receiving precisely calibrated messaging designed to exploit their specific vulnerabilities.

Let me give you some concrete examples of how this targeting can cause real harm. We've seen gambling companies use AI to follow people in addiction recovery around the internet, serving them ads at moments of weakness. Payday loan companies target families already struggling financially, using debt stress as a trigger for more debt products. There are serious concerns about children being exposed to content that isn't appropriate for them because age verification systems aren't foolproof. And mental health data – searches about depression, anxiety, or crisis support – can be used to target people in genuine distress with everything from miracle cures to predatory financial products. These aren't theoretical risks. They're documented cases where AI-powered precision targeting has been used in ways that cause genuine harm to people at their most vulnerable moments.

Now, you might wonder why people agree to this kind of targeting in the first place. The answer is that meaningful consent is rarely actually happening. Those lengthy terms and conditions documents? Most people don't read them, and the companies know that. The language is deliberately complex and legal, making it nearly impossible for ordinary users to understand what they're actually agreeing to. Even when people want to opt out, the processes are often buried deep in settings menus or designed to be confusing. What we call 'consent' in digital advertising is often just a legal fiction – a box-ticking exercise that protects companies while leaving users genuinely unaware of how their data will be used. True informed consent would require clear, simple explanations of exactly how personal information will be used for targeting, but that level of transparency is still rare in the industry.

So, what protections actually exist for consumers when it comes to AI-driven advertising? Quite a few, as it turns out. In the UK, legislation like the Online Safety Act and GDPR places real legal limits on how companies can use AI to target and profile you. You also have rights around opting out of personalised ads in many situations, so it is worth knowing those exist. Transparency is a big part of this too. Advertisers are increasingly expected to be upfront when AI has been used to create or tailor content. Beyond that, independent audits can play a really useful role in spotting bias or manipulative patterns in automated systems before they cause harm. And if something does go wrong, there should be clear routes for consumers to raise complaints and seek redress. The message here is that protection is possible, but it relies on strong rules, honest practice, and people knowing their rights.

So let us bring everything together from this lesson. The core idea is that AI-powered advertising is not random. It is precise, it is personalised, and it is shaped by data that you may not even realise you have shared. Because the systems are trained on imperfect data, bias can creep in quietly, without anyone deliberately choosing it. Personalisation might feel helpful, but it is never truly neutral – there are always choices being made about what you see and why. A useful habit to develop is simply asking yourself who stands to benefit when an advert feels particularly well-timed or persuasive. Finally, I would encourage you to take one practical step before the next lesson. Go into the settings on a platform you use regularly and have a look at what targeting preferences or data permissions are currently switched on. You might be surprised by what you find.

Who is this for: Marketing managers, advertising executives, digital marketers, social media managers, brand managers, communications specialists, content creators, media planners, campaign managers
Team or Dept this is for: Marketing, Communications, Digital Marketing, Brand Management, Media, Creative Services, Public Relations
